14 Souvenirs From Japan You Can't Return Home Without
# Meaningful Souvenirs From Japan You Can’t Return Home Without
ByAlyse
February 3, 2025February 28, 2025
Are you wondering about or are overwhelmed by the best souvenirs from Japan you should return with for your friends, family (or more importantly yourself)?
If you’re anything like me, you enjoy scouting out **meaningful souvenirs** from your adventures abroad to help spark a wonderful memory of your trip each time you look at it on your return home.
I’m not talking about mass-produced fridge magnets or ornaments made in China – I mean purchasing **authentic, thoughtful and hand-crafted **gifts from Japan that help support local jobs and businesses. After all, that’s what being an Invisible Tourist is all about!
![Meaningful Souvenirs from Japan: What to Buy in Japan | The Invisible Tourist](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/featured_94.jpg)
During my regular [trips to Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/japan-travel) over the past decade, I always come across many wonderful souvenirs and have amassed quite the collection. Some I use almost daily, while others I like to save for special occasions.
If you’d like to learn what to bring back from Japan, my suggested Japan souvenirs and where you can find them, read on for more!
*This post contains affiliate links, at no extra cost to you. I may make a small commission if you click through and make a purchase.*
![Handcrafted & Meaningful Souvenirs from Japan You Can't Return Home Without | The Invisible Tourist](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/07/japan-souvenirs-from-japan-what-to-buy-in-japan-pin7.jpg)*Pin me to Pinterest for reference later!*
## **Different types of top souvenirs from Japan**
When it comes to Japanese souvenirs the list is endless, really! There are so many beautiful things you can only buy in Japan. From everything Pokémon and Hello Kitty to electronics and unique beauty products, Japan has something for everyone.
As I mention in my [Tokyo itinerary](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/6-days-in-tokyo-itinerary-first-time/), “If you can’t find what you’re looking for in Tokyo, it probably doesn’t exist!”
However, my Japan souvenir guide focuses on **hand-crafted pieces** that are symbolic to the land of the rising sun and are sure to be admired by those you gift them to.
![Souvenirs from Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/souvenirs-from-japan-small-objects.jpg)
## Need help choosing what to buy in Japan?
Where do we start with the best gifts from Japan?! This is a country that has maintained its rich cultural heritage throughout history and many of the below souvenirs reflect this. It’s always good to know the significance behind each of the items you plan on purchasing as they become more meaningful gifts.
To get you inspired and to know what to keep an eye out for when you’re souvenir shopping in Japan, here are some of my personal favourites:
## **Wooden Souvenirs from Japan**
### **Tsuko-Tegata**
*Tsuko-tegata* 通行手形 or “wooden passports”represent an ancient custom in Japan. During the Edo period, travel between the different regions of the country was strictly monitored. To allow passage from one provence to another, travellers needed to present a tsuko-tegata to prove they had authorisation to pass through.
Today, *tsuko-tegata* can be found at various temples, shrines, hot spring resorts and are a symbol or “proof” that you have visited. They have a bell and braided string attached. Pictured below are mine from Istukushima Shrine (Miyajima, left) and Nara (right). As a typography lover, I couldn’t resist the hand-painted calligraphy… Isn’t it simply gorgeous?
While I have some *tsuko-tegata* on permanent display at home, I save others as decorations for my Christmas tree each year. For me, Christmas is a time of reflecting and appreciating all the good things from the year(s) that have passed and these pieces represent a journey and fond memories.
![Tsuko-tegata (wooden passports) from Miyajima Island (left) and Nara (right)](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/tsuko-tegata-wooden-japan-souvenir.jpg)
### **Lucky Charms**
Lucky charms are some of the best Japanese souvenirs! While *[omamori](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omamori)* お守り is a general term for Japanese lucky charms, they come in different kinds. These pocket-sized items are very popular with both locals and tourists alike. With origins from both Shintoism and Buddhism, their purpose is to drive away evil spirits with the attached bells and guard their owners from misfortune.
Spiritual leaders thought the idea of symbolically placing power of the gods into these hanging items would help protect believers and bring good luck.
Today there are *omamori* for almost everything you can think of, from the more traditional kinds such as prosperity and happiness to more modern variations for safe driving and cyber-security!
*Omamori* can also come in the form of small silk bags with a prayer inside. You can find *omamori* at various shrines, temples and shopping arcades throughout Japan. Pictured below are just a few pieces from my *omamori* collection:
* Blue pouch and rare porcelain *omamori* amulet from Japan’s porcelain capital, [Arita](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/arita-japan/)
* Yellow pouch from Senso-ji in Tokyo and the purple from Myoryu-ji in [Kanazawa](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/2-days-in-kanazawa-itinerary/)
* IT Card *omamori* from Kanda Shrine, Tokyo
* The fourth image below includes part of my *ema*(wooden wishing plaque), *omikuji* (paper fortune), *tsuko-tegata* (wooden passport) and *omamori* collection from temples and shrines all over Japan.
![Omamori from Tozan Shrine, Arita](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/omamori-tozan-shrine-arita.jpg)
![Japanese Souvenirs: Omamori](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/07/japanese-souvenirs-omamori.jpg)
![Omamori at Kanda Myojin, Tokyo](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/kanda-myojin-omamori.jpg)
![What to buy in Japan: Souvenirs collection of ema, omikuji, omamori & tsuko-tegata](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/what-to-buy-in-japan-souvenirs-collection.jpg)
### **Chopsticks**
Quite the obvious choice and one of the best Japan souvenirs, no doubt! I chose this pack of chopsticks in Hakone as each pair features a view of Mt Fuji by one of my favourite artists, Hokusai. You’re sure to recognise his work “The Great Wave off Kanagawa.”
Even though Mt Fuji was hiding behind the clouds during my visit to Hakone, these chopsticks were a nice reminder to me that I did make the journey to her home and they inspired me to return again.
**> NOTE:
**>
> Luckily for me, I DID make it back to see her with unobstructed blue skies on a separate occasion during my
[> Tokyo to Mt Fuji day trip!
](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/tokyo-to-mt-fuji-day-trip-itinerary/)
![Colourful chopsticks from Hakone](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/japanese-chopsticks-from-hakone.jpg)
READ MORE:
[Stunning Destinations to Discover in Japan Off the Beaten Path](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/japan-off-the-beaten-path-places-less-travelled/)
[20+ Essential Tips for Planning a Trip to Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/planning-a-trip-to-japan-travel-tips/)
[Kyoto Hidden Gems You Won’t Want to Miss](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/kyoto-hidden-gems/)
[Secret Tokyo Hidden Gems You’ve Never Heard Of](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/off-the-beaten-path-tokyo-hidden-gems/)
[Inspiring Day Trips from Osaka to Enrich Your Visit](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/best-day-trips-from-osaka/)
## **Homeware Souvenirs from Japan**
### **Ceramics & Pottery**
Did you know Japan is home to some of the oldest ceramic traditions in the world? There is evidence to suggest Japanese pottery and ceramics (*tōgei* 陶芸) were created as early as 10,000 BC during the Jōmon period.
Throughout the centuries the Japanese have successfully mastered this art form, which includes glazed pottery, stoneware and porcelain items. The quality and attention to detail is a level above the rest!
Sake sets, bowls, matcha bowls, dishes, mugs, cups, teapots, plates and the like are popular homeware souvenirs to buy in Japan.
I especially love these hand-crafted mugs I purchased at a small family-run store in Kyoto. The gold foil touches are exquisite and make me feel a little bit special each time I enjoy my tea.
**> TIP:
**> Some of the best places to buy pottery in Japan are
[> Kurashiki
](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/kurashiki-bikan-historical-quarter-japan/)> , Arita, Kanazawa, Kyoto and Mashiko.
![](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/matcha-after.jpg)
![Pottery and Ceramic Japanese Souvenirs from Kanazawa](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/07/japanese-souvenir-mugs.jpg)
![Hand-made ceramic mugs from Kyoto](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/japanese-ceramic-mugs.jpg)
### **Maneki Neko**
You’ve probably seen *maneki neko* if you enter an Asian store or restaurant, as they are one of the [many things Japan is famous for](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/what-is-japan-famous-for/). Have you ever wondered what these cute little cats mean and why you can find them so widespread? Hand-painted “fortune cats” have an interesting backstory and there’s a reason why they’re so popular!
Legend has it there was a man seeking shelter from rain beneath a tree when a cat appeared nearby. It raised its left paw, beckoning him over. As the man approached the cat lightening struck the tree, causing it to come crashing down where he was sitting. Hence, the cats are also known as “beckoning cats”.
**> TIP:
**>
> In Tokyo, you can actually visit the temple these cats are said to have originated from. Take a look at my guide to
[> Tokyo’> s hidden gems
](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/off-the-beaten-path-tokyo-hidden-gems/)> for all the info!
*Maneki neko* have different meanings depending on their colour. A white cat invites happiness, prosperity and positive things. Black *maneki neko* are said to ward off evil spirits. In addition, their raised paws symbolise different wishes.
If you’re hoping to attract customers to your store, look for a *maneki neko* raising its left paw. A right paw raised means your cat is inviting good fortune and money your way. A golden *maneki neko* is said to be one of the luckiest of Japanese souvenirs!
Even if you’re more of a dog person like me, it’s hard to resist these adorable lucky charms. They may even be the cutest souvenirs from Japan!
![](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/gotokuji-2.jpg)
![Maneki Neko, "Lucky Cats" from Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/maneko-neko-japanese-lucky-fortune-cat.jpg)
## **Artwork Souvenirs from Japan**
Do you have an art fanatic in your life? If so, you really can’t go wrong with Japanese artwork. As a massive art lover myself, I cannot resist buying artwork from local artists on my travels!
I have many pieces that all take pride of place in my home and make me smile each time I look at them. **Paintings** and traditional **woodblock prints** make good souvenirs from Japan as they’re super easy to take home with you – being nice and flat means they won’t take up much room in your suitcase.
As I was walking along the[Philosopher’s Path in Kyoto](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/4-days-in-kyoto-itinerary-first-time/), a local lady was sitting beneath the shade of a tree painting these cute little postcards by hand. She had quite a few on display in front of her for passers-by to admire. For only JPY 200 each, I chose three but now I wish I had selected more:
![Watercolour paintings by a local artist along the Philosopher's Path, Kyoto](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/kyoto-paintings-1.jpg)
Also, the beautiful board-mounted watercolours below are actually from the **gift shop at Kinkaku-ji** (Golden Pavilion) in Kyoto. Let me tell you, this is not your average, tacky gift shop you’d expect in other destinations – almost all the items for sale were locally sourced, unique pieces all of a very high standard.
Although the watercolours I chose were not cheap, I didn’t mind at all because I:
1. Fell in love with the paintings the moment I saw them;
2. Knew from the back of each painting that I had purchased a piece from a local Kyoto artist, and;
3. Was also helping to support the upkeep of the temple and gardens with my purchase.
Win-win for all!
![Stunning watercolour souvenirs from Kinkaku-ji](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/kyoto-paintings-2.jpg)
READ MORE:
[How to Find Hidden Bars in Kyoto Only Locals Know About](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/pub-crawl-kyoto-at-night/)
[What to Expect at a Traditional Kyoto Tea Ceremony](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/kyoto-tea-ceremony-japan/)
[The Best Time to Visit Japan for Cherry Blossoms](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/best-time-to-visit-japan-for-cherry-blossoms/)
## **Paper Souvenirs from Japan**
### **Folding Fans**
As an *invisible tourist*, I like to ensure the things I need when I’m out exploring foreign lands are small enough to fit in [my cross-body bag](https://amzn.to/2tq6yQY). Folding fans or *[sensu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_fan#Japanese_foldable_fan)*扇子 are no exception and come in handy in Japan’s summer heat! Their origins can be traced back to as early as the 6th century and are also used in tea ceremonies and traditional Japanese dances such as Kabuki.
Folding fans are very meaningful souvenirs from Japan and are usually made of bamboo or Japanese cyprus then connected by washi paper. Silk fans are considered the most precious. Did you know *sensu* are said to resemble life?
The wooden strips begin at one starting point, which you could say is birth, and slowly branch out along many different paths throughout life’s journey. Prosperity is also closely associated with these fans as their unfolding can be likened to that of a blooming flower or growing wealth.
If you’re wondering what to buy in Japan, Japanese folding fans make popular birthday gifts! If you’re thinking about buying *sensu* for a special someone, consider the symbolism you’d like to bestow on them:
|
* Cherry blossoms represent a parent’s love
* Plum blossoms represent a new beginning
* Pairs of birds represent loving couples
* Cranes and tortoises represent a long life|
* Koi fish represent a long and lucky life
* Bamboo and pine represent patience
* Lions represent strength
* Two butterflies represent a happy marriage|
My *sensu* is a screen-printed version of Hokusai’s “The Great Wave Off Kanagawa”. I just couldn’t leave Japan without a souvenir adorned with his iconic artwork!
![Types of paper souvenirs from Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/japan-paper-souvenirs.jpg)
READ MORE:
[6 Unique Experiences in Takayama Old Town By a Local](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/things-to-do-in-takayama-old-town/)
[Hida No Sato Folk Village: Takayama’s Delightful Crown Jewel](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/hida-no-sato-folk-village-takayama/)
### **Daruma Dolls**
Usually hollow and made of papier-mâché, Daruma dolls are appear to be fearsome bearded men. Their grumpy-looking demeanour actually has a positive message behind it. Believe it or not, they aren’t meant to be scary, but encouraging!
In Japanese culture, their rounded shape has been linked to the phrase “seven times down, eight times up”, which symbolises having a fighting spirit to overcome adversity and misfortune. Daruma dolls are usually coloured red with a white face and decorated with gold details. It’s said these interesting little characters are modelled on Bodhidharma, the founder of Zen and Buddhism.
If you look very closely, you’ll see some Daruma’s eyebrows are actually cranes facing one another and its beard is made up of tortoise shapes, both symbols of longevity in Japan.
Daruma dolls make great Japan souvenirs as they represent perseverance and good fortune. You may notice Daruma dolls have blank eyes at first, but this is to help encourage the recipient to achieve their goals. On receiving a Daruma doll, its owner can set a goal by drawing a pupil on the Daruma’s left eye (right eye facing you).
Once they have achieved the goal, they can then fill in the right blank eye. In the meantime, the single blank eye serves to remind its owner to persevere to achieve their goal and overcome any obstacles along the way.
**> TIP:
**>
> If you enjoy motivational expressions from Japan, don’t miss my detailed guide to
[> beautiful words in Japanese
](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/beautiful-japanese-words-meanings/)> and their meanings!
![](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/09/daruma-san.jpg)
### **Other paper souvenir items**
#### **Japanese Stationery**
Japan is literally a stationery-junkie’s paradise! From assorted [washi papers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Washi), washi tapes, stickers, notebooks and the like you’re sure to find specially crafted paper items for you or family and friends to treasure. Every cool Japanese souvenirs list includes their stationery, and it’s easy to see why – the amazing variety and quality is of a very high standard!
#### **Origami**
Have you heard the story of Sadako Sasaki and the **thousand paper cranes**? She was two years old when the American atomic bomb was dropped on her hometown of Hiroshima in 1945. After about nine years Sadako and thousands of survivors developed leukaemia and other illnesses directly related to radiation from the bomb.
Japanese legend says that one shall be granted a wish if they fold 1,000 paper cranes, so she began folding. Her one wish was to live, which unfortunately did not come true despite exceeding the thousand cranes. Sadako’s story actually inspired the **Children’s Peace Memorial in Hiroshima** (I highly recommend visiting!) I was able to bring back a small paper crane from the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum, which serves as a reminder of my visit to this significant city.
#### **Tickets & Maps**
This may seem pretty obvious but all those tickets and maps you collect during your visit to Japan are great little souvenirs! But, **what to do with them** when you return home? From DIY projects to scrapbooking to permanently displaying them in a meaningful way in your home, the possibilities are almost endless. Head over to [my Pinterest page](https://www.pinterest.com.au/theinvisibletourist) for some ideas and inspiration!
![Origami in Hiroshima](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/hiroshima-origami.jpg)
READ MORE:
[What No One Tells You About Visiting Nagano’s Snow Monkeys in Summer](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/jigokudani-monkey-park-in-summer/)
[20+ Phrases in Japanese for Tourists (+ FREE PDF Cheat Sheet!)](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/japanese-for-tourists/)
## **Textile Souvenirs from Japan**
### **Furoshiki Cloths**
Furoshiki are usually a square textile cloth adorned with beautiful patterns. Literally translated, Furoshiki means “bath spread” – I’ll get to why in a moment!
As a very versatile item, these cloths have had many uses since they were first popular during the Nara period, around 700BC – so their uses date back a lonnnng time.
From transporting treasures discovered in Japanese temples, to carrying bento boxes and even wrapping clothes in bath houses to avoid confusion… The uses for these cloths are almost endless.
By also using the cloth as a bath mat, these cloths got their name as *Furo* means “bathing” and *Shiki* means “standing on a rug”.
With the invention of luggage and suitcases in the 20th century, furoshiki became less popular and almost forgotten in Japan. However, these days they’re making a comeback as people are looking for more sustainable and eco-friendly alternatives to gift wrapping. And what a great history they have! Why not wrap your Japanese souvenir gifts in furoshiki cloth?
**> TIP:
**> Love kimono fabrics? Keep your eyes peeled for items made from upcycled kimono, such as pouches, handbags, hair accessories and more. Nippori Fabric Town in Tokyo is popular amongst visitors who love sewing!
![Furoshiki Cloths from Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/furoshiki-cloths.jpg)
![What to Buy in Japan: Furoshiki Cloths](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/07/what-to-buy-in-japan-furoshiki-cloth.jpg)
## **Japan souvenirs to last a lifetime**
### **Japanese cooking class**
Treat yourself or someone you know to a souvenir that keeps on giving – an authentic Japanese cooking class. Not only do [cultural experiences in Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/japan-cultural-experiences-activities/) like this help out local businesses, learning to cook a delicious dish from a local is a lesson to stay with you for life!
I’m the first to admit I’m no chef but I had an absolute blast during my authentic [ramen cooking class in Kyoto](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/kyoto-cooking-class-japan/). I learnt how to make this incredible chicken ramen from scratch (including punching the noodle dough no less that 100 times to get it perfect). It definitely gave me a whole new appreciation for ramen.
My favourite part (aside from eating) was getting to customise my own broth flavour. I chose a special white miso paste exclusive to the Kyoto region, of course. It was honestly the best ramen I’ve ever had and I couldn’t believe I’d made it myself!
[**For prices and to book my ramen cooking class in Kyoto, click here**](https://airkitchen.me/kitchen/77.php/?utm_source=theInvisibletourist&utm_medium=affiliate)
[**For more cooking classes in cities throughout Japan, click here.**](https://airkitchen.me/?utm_source=theInvisibletourist&utm_medium=affiliate)
![](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/07/kyoto-cooking-class7.jpg)
![](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/07/kyoto-cooking-class12.jpg)
### ***Sampuru* replica food making class**
Do you love Japanese replica food that’s on display in restaurants and cafes? Well, it’s possible to take a class and make your own to take home! Known as *sampuru *サンプル (sample food), I was able to make a ramen bowl that looked good enough to eat.
The class was so fun and I had a great time getting to learn more about the long history of this part of Japanese culture, and how it’s become so popular. Read my detailed review [how to make fake Japanese food](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/make-fake-japanese-food-sampuru/) in a top *sampuru* factory in Tokyo, and [book your own class here](https://www.klook.com/en-AU/activity/47396-Middle-Size-Food-Sample-Making-Experience-Tokyo/?aid=3398)!
![Sample Food Making in Tokyo](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/japanese-sample-food-making.jpg)
![How to make fake Japanese food in Tokyo](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/fake-japanese-food-ramen-sampuru.jpg)
## **What to buy in Japan: BONUS items**
### **Sweet Treats**
As an exception to other items I’ve mentioned so far as these are obviously mass-produced, **Japanese Kit-Kats** are especially unique snacks from Japan because the flavour combinations are insane! From matcha (green tea) to cherry blossom, soy bean to sake, wasabi to miso, you’re bound to find a flavour that makes you curious enough to want to give it a try.
Some other things to bring back from Japan snack-wise? In Japan, gifts to others at home from a destination are called *omiyage* (or *pasalubong* for our friends in the Philippines). These Japan souvenir ideas include:
* Tokyo Banana
* Tokyo Honey Sugar Butter Cookies
* Edo Musubisen
* Any regional-themed items as they are especially unique and make meaningful gifts for the sweet-tooth folk in your life (or for yourself to gorge on and reminisce about your trip when you get home)!
I’ve written a dedicated guide to what [snacks from Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/snacks-from-japan/) to bring home, including all the amazing types of Kit-Kats. It details the popular and traditional snacks, their fascinating histories and exactly where to find them. Did yours make the list?
**> Did you know?
**> Kit-Kats make popular gifts to wish students luck before exams in Japan. Every January (exam time) Nestlé noticed a surge in the sales of Kit-Kats. Turns out this was because Kit-Kat in Japanese sounds like the phrase
*> kitto-katsu
*> . When translated this means “to surely win” so customers wanted to pass this luck onto their loved ones!
![Tokyo Banana is one of the iconic Japanese snacks](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/tokyo-banana-kit-kat.jpg)
![Chestnut, Strawberry Tiramisu and Strawberry Cheesecake Kit Kat are just some flavours found in Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/kit-kats-from-japan.jpg)
### **Umbrellas**
Umbrellas are actually pretty cool souvenirs from Japan. I know this seems like a pretty random souvenir but I have to admit the quality of these umbrellas are almost unmatched. The “Waterfront” brand of umbrellas are especially awesome – their larger umbrellas have 24-prongs that make the umbrella super sturdy and also give them that traditional Japanese look.
Waterfront even make a pocket/handbag sized umbrella that folds down to only 22cm x 6cm x 2.5cm for about JPY 1000. Perfect for us [invisible tourists](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/traveller-stereotypes-invisible-tourist-part-1/) who like to fit things in our small crossbody bags! The clear plastic umbrellas you can buy from convenience stores for about JPY 500 are also very good quality.
## Where to go souvenir shopping in Japan
Aside from the temples I’ve mentioned above, you’ll be able to find loads of locally-made traditional souvenirs from Japan in the following locations. These souvenirs are rarely seen outside of the country so be sure to **save some room** for them in your suitcase!
**> TIP:
**> Pssst, if you need help with your itinerary planning, be sure to check out my detailed guide to spending
[> 2 weeks in Japan
](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/2-weeks-in-japan-itinerary-first-time/)> or wander of the beaten path by spending
[> 3 weeks in Japan
](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/japan-3-week-itinerary-off-the-beaten-track/)> , or use my
[> honeymoon itinerary for Japan
](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/japan-honeymoon-itinerary/)> if celebrating a special occasion!
### **Souvenirs from Tokyo**
* **Asakusa:** Nakamise-dori, the long street lined with shops and stalls leading up to the grand Senso-ji temple. It’s one of the most popular [shopping streets in Tokyo](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/streets-in-tokyo/). Here you’ll find lots of local food specialties, paper lanterns, umbrellas, lucky charms and even Samurai swords! Make your entrance onto this iconic street via the Thunder Gate.
* **Yanaka Ginza:** Perfect for purchasing artisan items such as knives, blades, ceramics and more, my guide to [Old Town Tokyo](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/old-town-tokyo-yanaka/) has the details.
* **Tokyo Station:** Famous for Tokyo Banana and loads of other unique Japanese treats!
* **Harajuku:** Takeshita-dori for quirky souvenirs and lucky charms. On Omote-sando, Oriental Bazaar is your one-stop shop for authentic Japanese goods such as kimonos, antiques, homewares and fans to name a few.
* **Nakano Broadway:** For the anime fans, this alternative to Akihabara is a great place to find your favourite merch!
* **These places are mentioned in my **[**6 Days in Tokyo Itinerary**](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/6-days-in-tokyo-itinerary-first-time/)** if you’re interested in finding out more!**
### **Souvenirs from Kyoto**
* **Downtown: **Sanjo Meitengai Shopping Arcade. This is where I found my gorgeous mugs I mentioned above. There are 7kms of shopping arcades that run parallel to Kawaramachi-dori and criss-cross between Sanjo-dori and Shijo-dori – you’re sure to find what you’re after!
* **Gion:** The gorgeous Sannenzaka & Ninnenzaka streets, leading up the hill to Matsubara-dori and Kiyomizu-dera Temple. There are many traditional wooden specialty stores along these streets that sell folding fans, snacks, green tea and other goodies. On the way back down from Kiyomizu-dera, head down Chawan-zaka or “Teapot Lane” if you’re after beautiful Japanese tea sets.
* **These places are mentioned in my **[**4 Days in Kyoto Itinerary**](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/4-days-in-kyoto-itinerary-first-time/)** if you’re interested in finding out more!**
![The astonishing preserved streets of Sannenzaka & Ninnenzaka, Gion, Kyoto](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/05/sannenzaka_ninnenzaka.jpg)
### **Souvenirs from Osaka**
* ****Shinsaibashi-suji**Shopping Street**is one of Osaka’s oldest shopping destinations. You’ll find a mix of independent boutiques along with chain storeshere. Be sure to keep an eye out for matcha (green tea) items as these are popular souvenirs from Japan.
* **Shinsekai:** This Showa-era neighbourhood is a great place to find knives and traditionally-made snacks. My [guide to Osaka street food](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/osaka-street-food/) has the details.
* **These places are mentioned in my complete [3 Days in Osaka Itinerary](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/3-days-in-osaka-itinerary-nara-day-trip/) if you’d like to find out more!**
### **Souvenirs from Hiroshima**
* **Hon-dori Shopping Arcade** for okinomiyaki-flavoured crackers (a type of Japanese omelette originating in Hiroshima), momiji manju (maple leaf cakes with various flavours of custard fillings) or rice scoop paddles, which are traditional handicrafts from this region and can be used as good luck charms or displaying in your home. All these items and more can be found here!
* **This is mentioned in my complete [things to do in Hiroshima](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/things-to-do-in-hiroshima-attractions/) guideif you’d like to find out more!**
### **Souvenirs from Miyajima**
* Pick up a few **souvenirs from Omotesando street**. I bought a lovely a furoshiki cloth, tsuko-tegata and delicious sweets unique to the Hiroshima region such asMomiji-manju here.
* **This is mentioned in my guide from [Hiroshima to Miyajima by ferry](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/hiroshima-to-miyajima-day-trip-ferry/)!**
### **Souvenirs from Takayama**
* Buy fabrics and textiles, wooden items, *Sarubobo* dolls and much more at **Miyagawa Morning Market**. Sake can be found in the [Sanmachi Suji (Old Town) area](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/things-to-do-in-takayama-old-town/). There are also plenty of locally hand-crafted souvenirs at nearby [Hida no Sato](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/hida-no-sato-folk-village-takayama/).
* **These places are mentioned in my complete [2 Days in Takayama Itinerary](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/2-days-in-takayama-itinerary/) if you’d like to find out more!**
![Hondori Shopping Arcade, Hiroshima](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/hondori-shopping-arcade-hiroshima.jpg)
## **Need more inspiration for Japan trip planning?**
Now you know the best things to buy in Japan, I can also help get your itinerary planning off to a good start by sharing my [tips for visiting Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/planning-a-trip-to-japan-travel-tips/) as well as helpful insider info so you can make the most of your experience! Tailored especially for first-time visitors, these are my tried-and-tested personal itineraries I’ve used during my trips.
They each cover **things to do, costs, getting around, what to eat and recommendations for where to stay**.
Additionally, find out what you need to [pack for Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/what-to-pack-for-japan/), do’s and don’ts of [Japanese cultural etiquette](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/dos-and-donts-in-japan-tourist-guide-etiquette/)and more on [my full travel blog for Japan](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/japan-travel)!
![Ceramics from Japan | The Invisible Tourist](https://www.theinvisibletourist.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/ceramics-from-japan-1.jpg)
## Concluding what to buy in Japan
Of course, these are just a snapshot of meaningful Japanese souvenirs to help you remember your trip. From wooden items to artworks and ceramics to paper goods, Japan’s rich and unique history is evident in each piece. It feels good knowing that your**spending goes back into local jobs and businesses**.
Obviously there are thousands of additional types of souvenirs in Japan you can find which can be a bit overwhelming (like different types of sake and mochi). But, I really wanted this souvenir guide to focus on the more** traditional, hand-crafted items** that are special and share the significance behind them.
What are your thoughts on these Japan souvenir suggestions? Which pieces are your favourite or has this helped you decide what to buy in Japan? I hope you found this gift guide inspiring! If you did, I’d love if you could share it with your fellow Japan lovers. You’re also welcome to join me on [Facebook,](https://www.facebook.com/theinvisibletourist/)[Pinterest,](https://au.pinterest.com/invisibltourist/japan-the-invisible-tourist/)[Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/theinvisibletourist/)and[TikTok](https://www.tiktok.com/@theinvisibletourist)for more travel tips and inspiration!
Until next time,
